These can be shared with the school community to inspire others and reinforce the unit's focus on leadership roles and community impact.","url":""},{"t":"Seesaw","d":"Facilitate a digital leadership reflection journal on Seesaw, where students regularly post voice recordings, videos, or annotated images about their own leadership actions and observations in the school. This ongoing portfolio supports self-management and connects directly to the unit's emphasis on personal growth and responsibility as a leader.","url":""},{"t":"Book Creator","d":"Guide students to collaboratively author a digital book titled 'Leaders in Our Community', combining research, interviews, and creative writing. Each student contributes a chapter profiling a leader (local, national, or global), explicitly linking their findings to the roles, responsibilities, and qualities explored in the unit.","url":""},{"t":"Adobe Express Podcasting","d":"Students launch a 'Leadership Voices' podcast, interviewing community leaders and reflecting on the qualities that shape effective leadership. Using Adobe Express Podcasting (https://podcast.adobe.com), they script, record, and edit episodes that explore how leadership impacts communities, fostering authentic connections and communication skills.","url":""},{"t":"Leadership Robots: Guiding Our Community","d":"Students form small 'leadership teams' to design and physically build a miniature community using recycled materials, cardboard, and craft supplies?featuring key spaces like a town hall, park, school, and homes. Teams use the Design Cycle to empathize with community needs, ideate solutions, prototype their community layout and leadership pathways, test their robot's effectiveness as a leader, and refine both the physical build and coding. The project culminates in a showcase where teams explain how their robot's programmed 'leadership qualities' (e.g., clear communication, decision-making, empathy) helped shape and support their community.","url":""}]

humanVerifiedSuggestionSignature: [{"t":"Canva","d":"Transform your Year 5 students into media creators by having them design persuasive advertisements or campaign posters in Canva. They'll experiment with colour, layout, and persuasive language, reflecting on how their choices influence beliefs and behaviours. This hands-on, creative process deepens their understanding of media manipulation and audience targeting, while building essential digital literacy and design skills.","url":"https://www.canva.com/"},{"t":"Adobe Express Podcasting","d":"Students become media creators by scripting, recording, and editing persuasive audio ads and interviews targeting specific audiences using Adobe Express Podcasting (https://podcast.adobe.com). This hands-on experience brings critical media literacy to life and gives students a powerful platform to share their voices.","url":""},{"t":"Podcast Equipment & GarageBand","d":"Students create a podcast series where they interview community members or experts about how media messages have influenced their beliefs or actions. Each machine features buttons, levers, or sensors that trigger lights, sounds, or moving parts programmed to convey a persuasive message (e.g., environmental awareness, healthy eating). Students prototype, test, and refine both the physical build and digital code, reflecting on how design choices (visuals, interactivity, language, effects) influence audience response. The final arcade is set up for peers to experience and evaluate the impact of each machine's message.","url":""}]

ci: Design processes enable people to respond creatively to challenges in the natural world.

humanVerifiedSuggestionSignature: [{"t":"Merge Cubes","d":"Students design interactive 3D models of natural hazards using Merge Cubes and bring them to life with Delightex, allowing peers to explore and annotate features such as volcanoes, earthquakes, or flood zones. This immersive app smash deepens understanding of how design processes respond to challenges in the natural world, as learners manipulate and present their models in augmented reality. The experience culminates in a virtual showcase where students explain their creative solutions and scientific thinking.","url":""},{"t":"Wise Discussion Chatbots","d":"Learners engage with a teacher-created Wise Discussion Chatbot using the Socratic Tutor scenario, where the chatbot takes on the role of 'Dr Maya Chen, a disaster resilience engineer'. After the dialogue, each student produces a claim-evidence-reasoning response summarising how design thinking can reduce the impact of natural hazards.","url":""},{"t":"Minecraft Education","d":"Students collaborate in Minecraft: Moon Base One (https://education.minecraft.net/en-us/lessons/moon-base-one) to design and build a sustainable lunar base that responds to natural challenges like meteor impacts, extreme temperatures, and resource scarcity. Teams capture screenshots and brief explanations of their base?s hazard-resilient features for a class gallery walk.","url":""},{"t":"Lego Spike Prime","d":"Teams engineer and program Lego Spike Prime robots to simulate emergency response vehicles navigating through model disaster zones. Learners apply design processes to build, code, and test their robots? routes, reflecting on how creative engineering can solve challenges posed by natural hazards. They combine photos of prototypes, audio reflections, diagrams, and data visualisations to capture each stage of their process, culminating in a digital portfolio to share with peers and the wider community.","url":""},{"t":"Sphero BOLT","d":"Teams investigate a specific natural hazard and use the Design Cycle to sketch, plan, and construct a disaster-resilient mini-home prototype. Students program a Sphero BOLT to simulate a 'family' navigating hazard scenarios (e.g., shaking for earthquakes, moving to higher ground for floods), testing and refining their designs against teacher-created events. The project culminates in a showcase where teams pitch their homes, demonstrating both engineering and coding solutions.","url":""}]

ci: Finding peaceful solutions to conflict can make a difference in the world.

humanVerifiedSuggestionSignature: [{"t":"Merge Cubes","d":"Learners create 3D conflict scenario dioramas on Merge Cube, then use Delightex to animate peaceful resolutions and dialogue between characters. They analyse the collected data to identify common causes and impacts of conflict, then collaboratively create a summary report that highlights key findings and suggests strategies for conflict resolution aligned with the unit?s central idea.","url":""},{"t":"Adobe Express Podcasting","d":"Students create a 'Peace Talks' podcast, analysing protest songs, historical events, and personal stories of conflict resolution. Using Adobe Express Podcasting (https://podcast.adobe.com), they collaborate to script and record episodes that explore peaceful solutions to conflict, amplifying diverse perspectives and fostering empathy.","url":""},{"t":"Canva","d":"Students will work in teams to create an interactive digital 'Peace Pathways' infographic in Canva. Each group will research a real-world conflict (local or global), identify its causes and impacts, and then design a visually engaging flowchart that maps out peaceful conflict resolution strategies. Students will incorporate protest song lyrics, quotes from historical peacemakers, and their own reflections. The final Canva project will include clickable elements linking to student-created audio reflections or short video explanations, making the resource both informative and interactive. This empowers students to synthesise their learning and share actionable pathways to peace with the wider school community.","url":"https://www.canva.com/"},{"t":"Minecraft Education","d":"Students collaboratively explore the 'Peace Builders' Minecraft Education world (https://education.minecraft.net/en-us/lessons/peace-builders), engaging with stories of Nobel Peace Laureates and designing their own virtual spaces that promote dialogue, negotiation, and peaceful solutions to conflict. They reflect on how these digital environments can inspire real-world action.","url":""},{"t":"Peace Pathways: Interactive Conflict Resolution Board","d":"Students collaboratively design and build a large, physical board game that simulates real-world conflict scenarios and peaceful resolution strategies. Using cardboard, recycled materials, clay, and art supplies, they craft the game board, event cards, and player pieces representing diverse cultures and perspectives. Integrating Sphero Indi robots or similar, students program these robots to act as 'peacemakers' that move along the board based on players' decisions and outcomes. As part of the Design Cycle, students empathise by researching global and local conflicts, define key issues, ideate peaceful solutions, prototype their board and tech integrations, test play with classmates, and refine both physical and digital elements. This hands-on, hybrid project fosters creativity, critical thinking, and empathy by blending tangible making with interactive technology.","url":""}]

lo: The impact of colonisation; Colonisation stories; The effects of colonisation ??? past, present and future

They analyse multiple perspectives by plotting historical events and cultural sites, then present their findings as interactive map stories that reveal how colonisation has shaped society across time and place.","url":""},{"t":"Canva","d":"Students create interactive timelines or digital storyboards that map out the stories of colonisation from multiple perspectives?Indigenous, colonisers, and contemporary voices. They can include audio narration, historical images, and reflective questions to deepen understanding.","url":""},{"t":"Physical Makerspace Project: Colonisation Impact Diorama","d":"Students design and build a 3D diorama using cardboard, recycled materials, fabric, and natural objects to represent a place before, during, and after colonisation. ci: People's choices are influenced by their beliefs and values and can impact others.
lo: How values and beliefs impact choices; How people's choices and actions impact others; Responsible decision-making
humanVerifiedSuggestionSignature: [{"t":"Canva","d":"Students will create an interactive digital poster or infographic in Canva that explores how personal beliefs and values shape decision-making and the ripple effects of those choices on others. They will interview classmates or family members about a value-driven choice, then visually map out the perspectives, motivations, and impacts using Canva's design tools. This creative process encourages empathy, critical thinking, and collaboration, while allowing students to reflect on their own values and how their actions influence their community and the world.","url":"https://www.canva.com/"},{"t":"Stop Motion Studio","d":"Students create stop-motion animations that illustrate the ripple effects of choices influenced by beliefs and values, bringing to life scenarios where decisions impact individuals and communities. They storyboard, film, and edit their sequences to demonstrate responsible decision-making and empathy, producing engaging visual stories that deepen understanding of the unit?s central idea.","url":""},{"t":"Adobe Express","d":"Students will create an interactive digital story using Adobe Express, exploring a real-life scenario where personal beliefs and values influence decision-making. Each student will design a 'Ripple Effect' visual journey: starting with a single choice, they will map out the cascading impacts on individuals, communities, and even global contexts. By integrating images, text, and voice narration, students will reflect on how their own values shape their actions and how those actions can inspire positive change. This project encourages empathy, critical thinking, and creativity, making the abstract concept of 'Who We Are' tangible and memorable.","url":"https://express.adobe.com/"},{"t":"Wise Discussion Chatbots","d":"The teacher creates a Wise Discussion Chatbot using the Character Interview scenario and sets the bot's role as Ava, a community celebration organiser explaining why particular traditions matter to different families. The topic is ?Who We Are: People's choices are influenced by their beliefs and values and can impact others.?. Students chat with the bot to explore a named perspective through guided questioning rather than general research; for example, they ask ?How does this celebration show what your community values?? and ?What might outsiders misunderstand about this tradition??. After the chat, students produce a question bank plus a short perspective-and-evidence summary that compares the chatbot responses with unit sources linked explicitly to Who We Are.","url":""},{"t":"Minecraft Education","d":"Students explore the stories of Nobel Peace Laureates in 'Minecraft: Active Citizen' (https://education.minecraft.net/en-us/lessons/active-citizen), then reflect on how these individuals' beliefs and values led to choices that impacted others. Students can build digital monuments or scenes representing key moments of responsible action.","url":""},{"t":"Values in Motion: Chain Reaction Empathy Machine","d":"Students work in teams to design and build a large Rube Goldberg-style chain reaction machine that physically represents how one person's choices and values can create ripple effects in a community. Using recycled materials (cardboard, tubes, marbles, dominoes, string, etc.), students construct interconnected segments, each symbolizing a belief, value, or action (e.g., kindness, honesty, sharing). At key points, teams integrate simple programmable robotics (such as Sphero Indi or Bee-Bots) to trigger or be triggered by physical mechanisms?bridging digital and physical worlds. The machine is mapped and documented digitally (using iPads for video, time-lapse, or simple animation overlays) to narrate the journey of an 'action' through the system. Students reflect on each segment's impact, fostering discussion on how values-driven decisions can set off positive or negative chains in real life. The project follows the Design Cycle: Inquire (research values and chain reactions), Develop Ideas (plan machine segments), Create (build and integrate tech), and Evaluate (test, reflect, iterate).","url":""}]

ci: Energy may be converted, transformed, and used to support human progress.
lo: Different forms of energy and how they are used; The impact of different energy sources on the planet; Sustainable energy practices
humanVerifiedSuggestionSignature: [{"t":"Merge Cubes","d":"Teams build virtual energy systems?like wind farms or solar grids?on Merge Cube, then use Delightex to simulate energy transformations and track their impact on human progress. This hands-on app smash lets students experiment with converting and storing energy, visualising invisible processes in 3D, and presenting their findings in an engaging, interactive format. The activity brings the science of energy to life and encourages creative problem-solving for sustainable futures.","url":""},{"t":"Micro:bit","d":"Students use Micro:bit to gather real-time data about energy use and experiment with simple data logging, directly connecting to the unit's focus on energy transformation and sustainability. (https://microbit.org/teach/lessons/?selected=energy-awareness)","url":""},{"t":"Minecraft Education","d":"Students explore the 'Minecraft: Alternative Energy' lesson to investigate hydropower, wind power, and waste-to-energy systems. Each student or group can include photos of their experiments (using the iPad camera), diagrams, voice recordings explaining energy conversions, and reflections on sustainable practices, resulting in a multimedia portfolio.","url":""},{"t":"Sphero BOLT","d":"Transform your energy unit by challenging students to design and code Sphero BOLT robots to demonstrate real-world energy transformations! The final creations are displayed in a 'Sustainable Energy Gallery Walk' where students explain the energy transformations their sculpture demonstrates and reflect on real-world applications.","url":""}]

(https://microbit.org/teach/lessons/?selected=energy-awareness)","url":""},{"t":"Eco-Action Interactive Dioramas","d":"Students work in teams to design and build a large-scale, multi-layered diorama showcasing a local ecosystem under threat (e.g., wetlands, bushland, or coastal area).
